elements.sent-2411-001: The recent lattice QCD calculations of the neutron and proton electric dipole moments (EDMs) and the CP-violating $\pi {\rm NN}$ coupling constant due to the $\theta$ term are reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-002: Progress towards nucleon EDM calculations, including the Weinberg three-gluon operator, and the quark chromoelectric dipole moment operator and their renormalization, is also discussed.
elements.sent-2411-003: The amount of nuclear matter we observe in the Universe must be the remnant of the imbalance between matter and antimatter during the evolution shortly after the Big Bang, commonly referred to as the baryonic asymmetry of the Universe (BAU).
elements.sent-2411-004: Observations indicate that the density of antimatter is much smaller than that of matter.
elements.sent-2411-005: For example, the antiproton to proton flux ratio is measured to be $\sim 10^{-4}$ by the AMS collaboration~\cite{AMS:2016oqu .}
elements.sent-2411-006: This asymmetry is also quantified by the ratio $\eta$, which is the net baryon density relative to the photon density: $\eta = \frac{n_b {n_{\gamma }$, where $n_b = n_B - n_{\overline{B }$ is the difference between the baryon density $n_B$ and the antibaryon density $n_{\overline{B }$, and $n_{\gamma}$ is the photon density.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: This ratio can be determined in two different ways: one from the abundance of light elements in the intergalactic medium (IGM) ~\cite{Fields:2019pfx , produced during the early universe in the process known as Big Bang nucleosynthesis (BBN) and the other from the power spectrum of temperature fluctuations in the cosmic microwave background (CMB)~\cite{Jungman:1995bz ; these methods yield consistent results.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The latest value from BBN is $\eta \approx 6 \times 10^{-10}$~\cite{Yeh:2022heq,ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Such a small baryon asymmetry poses one of the most outstanding problems facing cosmology, astrophysics and particle physics.
elements.sent-2411-010: According to Sakharov~\cite{Sakharov:1967dj , there are three necessary conditions for baryogenesis : I) baryon number violation, II) charge (C) and charge-parity (CP) violation, and III) a deviation from thermal equilibrium.}
elements.sent-2411-011: These conditions can be met in the standard model (SM).
elements.sent-2411-012: Conditions II) and III) are more straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-013: The weak interaction violates P-invariance maximally, while CP-invariance is violated by the complex phase $\delta_{CKM}$ in the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM)~\cite{Kobayashi:1973fv quark flavor-mixing matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The expansion of the Universe brings the primordial plasma out of thermal equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-015: On the other hand, condition I) can occur due to the quantum anomaly which breaks baryon number conservation~\cite{Adler:1969gk,Bell:1969ts .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, the strength of CP violation (CPV) in the CKM matrix is insufficient to explain baryogenesis~\cite{Shaposhnikov:1987tw,Farrar:1993hn,Gavela:1993ts,Huet:1994jb .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Similarly, CP violation due to the QCD $\theta$ term is also unlikely to produce the observed baryon asymmetry in the Universe (BAU)~\cite{Dolgov:1991fr .}
elements.sent-2411-018: These considerations have led to the study of beyond standard model (BSM) physic scenarios for BAU~\cite{Cohen:1993nk,Chupp:2017rkp .}
elements.sent-2411-019: It is feasible to examine the Sakarov condition II) experimentally at low energies and theoretically in terms of the electric dipole moment (EDM).
elements.sent-2411-020: The discovery of a nonzero value of the permanent electric dipole moment (EDM) of non-degenerate systems, such as elementary particles, atoms, or molecules would signal the existence of both time-reversal (T) and parity (P) non-invariance, which is equivalent to CP violation~\cite{Luders:1954zz .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The earliest experiment to search for the permanent neutron electric dipole moment (nEDM) aimed to test P non-invariance~\cite{Purcell:1950zz .}
elements.sent-2411-022: It was later realized that these experiments would also probe CP violation~\cite{Landau:1957tp,Lee:1973iz,Lee:1974jb .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Neutrons provide a convenient testing ground for EDM experimentally.
elements.sent-2411-024: Since neutrons do not carry electric charge, they can be subjected to large electric fields, either in a beam or in a bottle, without having their motion significantly affected.
elements.sent-2411-025: As spin 1/2 particles, neutrons can be readily spin-polarized using polarizing mirrors (in neutron beam) or superconducting magnets via the $\vec{\mu \cdot \vec{B}$ interaction (for ultracold neutrons (UCN)).}
elements.sent-2411-026: Searches for nEDM have been conducted in both beam and ultracold experiments with polarized neutrons.
elements.sent-2411-027: The latest measured value of nEDM from the ultracold neutron experiment is $d_n = (0.0 \pm 1.1_{\rm stat \pm 0.2_{\rm sys ) \times 10^{-26}\, {\rm e\cdot cm}$ ~\cite{Abel:2020gbr .}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Several ongoing experimental efforts include nEDM\@LANL~\cite{Ito:2017ywc , TUCAN~\cite{TUCAN:2018vmr , PanEDM~\cite{Wurm:2019yfj , PNPI nEDM~\cite{Serebrov:2017rby (these are UCN experiments), as well as beam nEDM~\cite{Chang:2018uxx (a pulsed neutron beam experiment).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: While they have different timelines, they all aim to increase statistics by a factor 10 to 100 by 2030.
elements.sent-2411-030: The electric dipole moment (EDM) of charged particles can be measured in storage rings.
elements.sent-2411-031: For example, the measurement of the electron EDM (eEDM) has been attempted using trapped molecular ions~\cite{Cairncross:2017fip , and there is a proposal to probe it in a storage ring~\cite{Suleiman:2021whz .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The Storage Ring COSY plans to hold protons and deuterons for their EDM measurements~\cite{CPEDM:2019nwp .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Atoms and molecules have served as sensitive platforms for precision measurements of symmetry violations for decades, including CP violation for EDMs~\cite{Chupp:2017rkp,Safronova:2017xyt .}
elements.sent-2411-034: To investigate hadronic CPV, diamagnetic systems are used, such as $^{129 {\rm Xe , ^{199 {\rm Hg}$ and $^{225 {\rm Ra}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: These experiments currently set the best limits on the eEDM, semileptonic CPV interactions, and quark chromo-EDMs.
elements.sent-2411-036: They are also competitive with nEDM experiments regarding sensitivity to quark EDMs and the QCD $\theta$ term.
elements.sent-2411-037: A summary of the recent experiments on EDMs of various systems and their bounds is provided in Ref.~\cite{Chupp:2017rkp .}
elements.sent-2411-038: There are two sources of CP violation in the standard model that contribute to the neutron electric dipole moment (nEDM).
elements.sent-2411-039: One is the CKM matrix in the weak interaction, which induces an EDM of the quarks at the two-loop order~\cite{Shabalin:1978rs,Shabalin:1982sg involving heavy quarks.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The recent results from the CKM matrix calculation of nEDM $\rm d_n$ and proton EDM (pEDM) $\rm{d_p}$ are \mbox{$1 \times 10^{-32}\, {\rm e \cdot cm < \{ \rm{|d_n (CKM)|, |d_p (CKM)|}\} < 6 \times 10^{-32}\, {\rm e \cdot cm}$}~\cite{Seng:2014lea .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This range is 5 to 6 orders of magnitude smaller than the current upper limit on the nEDM.
elements.sent-2411-042: Thus, nEDM experiments have a good discovery potential for BSM physics.
elements.sent-2411-043: The second source is the $\theta$-term in QCD, represented by the topological charge operator $-\bar{\theta \frac{g^2 {32\pi^2 G_{\mu\nu ^a \tilde{G _{\alpha\beta ^a$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The EDM induced by the CKM matrix is suppressed due to the flavor non-diagonal nature of quark flavor-mixing.
elements.sent-2411-045: The topological charge term is the only four-dimensional flavor-diagonal operator that can produce an EDM.
elements.sent-2411-046: If this is the sole contribution to the EDM, the current experimental limit on EDM implies that $\bar{\theta < 10^{-10}$ based on chiral symmetry considerations of a pion loop contribution~\cite{Crewther:1979pi,Baluni:1978rf .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In contrast to the CKM matrix where $\delta_{CKM}$ is of order unity, this small $\bar{\theta}$ is known as the strong CP problem.
elements.sent-2411-048: One proposed solution to address the strong CP problem is the introduction of the axion - a pseudo-Goldstone boson - via the Peccei-Quinn mechanism~\cite{Peccei:1977hh .}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this framework, $\bar{\theta}$ becomes a dynamical field with a potential minimized at $\bar{\theta = 0.$ The smallness of $\bar{\theta}$ can be understood as being dynamically relaxed~\cite{Peccei:1977ur,Peccei:1977hh,Weinberg:1977ma,Wilczek:1977pj , though generally not to exactly zero, due to effects that violate the Peccei-Quinn symmetry~\cite{Barr:1992qq,Kamionkowski:1992mf,Holman:1992us,Ghigna:1992iv .}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Another possibility is that there are BSM interactions responsible for CP violation, which can be classified as higher dimensional operators in effective theories.
elements.sent-2411-051: Some of these operators can mix with the four-dimensional topological term.
elements.sent-2411-052: The smallness of $\bar{\theta}$ would then reflect the large scale of BSM physics.
elements.sent-2411-053: While BAU and nEDM may require new beyond Standard Model (BSM) physics, relating nuclear and atomic EDMs to the high-energy CP violation mechanism will necessitate hadronic and nuclear inputs.
elements.sent-2411-054: Below the BSM scale, flavor-diagonal CP violation can be described by extending the Standard Model (SM) Lagrangian with gauge-invariant higher-dimensional operators, leading to a standard model effective field theory (SMEFT)~\cite{Buchmuller:1985jz,Grzadkowski:2010es,Cirigliano:2013lpa .}
elements.sent-2411-055: A list of operators relevant to CP violation can be found in~\cite{Engel:2013lsa .}
elements.sent-2411-056: To address hadron and nuclear systems below the electroweak scale, heavy SM degrees of freedom, such as W and Z bosons and the Higgs, can be integrated out by matching the SMEFT Lagrangian to a low-energy effective field theory (LEFT)~\cite{Jenkins:2017jig,Jenkins:2017dyc .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The complete one-loop matching calculation has been carried out~\cite{Dekens:2019ept .}
elements.sent-2411-058: A subset of operators relevant to EDM, estimated to have dominant contributions~\cite{deVries:2012ab , include the quark EDM (qEDM), the quark chromo-EDM (qcEDM), the gluon chromo-EDM (gcEDM) --- known as the Weinberg operator~\cite{Weinberg:1989dx --- and the four-fermion operators.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The discovery of an electric dipole moment (EDM) in any single system --- be it a hadron, a lepton or a molecule --- would represent a significant breakthrough and paradigm-shift.
elements.sent-2411-060: However, it would not be sufficient to definitely identify the source or discriminate among different BSM models.
elements.sent-2411-061: It is necessary to bound EDMs in complementary systems to identify the characteristic features of CP violation at low-energies and establish a connection to the underlying BSM origin.
elements.sent-2411-062: One crucial ingredient in this process is having reliable theoretical estimates of the relevant matrix elements for the aforementioned CP-violating operators.
elements.sent-2411-063: This is where lattice QCD calculations come into play.
elements.sent-2411-064: Over the last five decades, lattice QCD calculations based on the Euclidean path-integral formulation of QCD on a discrete space-time lattice~\cite{Wilson:1974sk have developed into a powerful tool for {\it ab initio calculations of strong-interaction physics.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: It is presently the only theoretical approach to solving QCD with controlled systematic errors.
elements.sent-2411-066: Many of the recent lattice ensembles have reached the physical pion mass, allowing for systematic errors in continuum and infinite volume extrapolations to be quantified, controlled, and improved.
elements.sent-2411-067: Lattice fermion formulations that accommodate chiral symmetry have been developed over the last three decades, including domain-wall fermions (DWF)~\cite{Kaplan:1992bt,Shamir:1993zy , overlap fermions~\cite{Neuberger:1997fp , and fixed-point fermions~\cite{Hasenfratz:1998jp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: In this review, we will discussion the progress on lattice calculations of the $\theta$ term, the qEDM, the qcEDM, and the Weinberg operator.
elements.sent-2411-069: A previous review on the subject has been given in \cite{Shindler:2021bcx .}
elements.sent-2411-070: This review serves as an update on the latest lattice results, particularly concerning the $\theta$ term and the CP-violating $\pi {\rm NN}$ coupling.
elements.sent-2411-071: We will not discuss the four-fermion operators as there are currently no lattice calculations for these operators.

elements.sent-2411-250: It represents the first Mellin moment of the collinear transversity parton distribution function (PDF) and can be extracted from the transversity parton distribution from deep inelastic scattering.

elements.sent-2411-287: We have observed significant improvements in the calculations of nEDM and pEDM with the $\theta$ term, despite the smallness of the signals.

elements.sent-2411-291: Lattice calculations of the Weinberg term and the quark chromo-electric dipole moment (qcEDM) have been undertaken.
elements.sent-2411-292: A major challenge for these operators is the complicated renormalization pattern.
elements.sent-2411-293: As higher dimensional operators, they mix with both other operators of the same dimension and lower-dimensional ones, leading to divergences in powers of $1/a$ in off-shell renormalization schemes, such as RI/MOM.
elements.sent-2411-294: The gradient flow with short flow-time expansion (SFTE) is gauge invariant and makes the renormalization of higher-dimensional operators more manageable.
elements.sent-2411-295: The power divergence of $1/a^2$ is reflected in the $1/t$ behavior in the SFTE as $t$ approaches zero.
elements.sent-2411-296: In contrast to the small signal of nEDM due to the $\theta$ term, which is $\sim 10^{-3}$, the nEDM from the Weinberg term and qcEDM appear to be on the order of $O(1)$ to $O(10)$.
